item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love that this explains SCP-1422. Loved it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If SCP-5700-7 did have telepathic abilities he could of made it easier for SCP-5700-1 to convince graham to not shoot . ( just an idea )

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Should this have the Eric tag. Also is Eric the reason Harry said run and why the foundation think he might have a second ability.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: e r i c ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i always seem to love the entries that get flak and dislike the entries that get gushed about, lol. I really enjoyed this. it was strong and engaging, with great character development and well written interviews. the people you describe are all distinct, which is awesome. the bit with Graham got a bit hokey/saccharine … but I liked it anyway. I wouldn't want it, or at least the essence of it and what it means, to be changed. it made me misty… it warmed my heart to see him sacrifice everything to do what's right —a rare kind of courage. I found Eric's letter a little corny, which gave a rather weak ending to what IMO was mostly a very strong entry.

a different fight, but harry gives me some john brown vibes. (ooo… I'd love to see a john brown inspired skip.)

an enthusiastic +1 from this person, anyway. I'm glad this was the last entry I read. :-)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought this was pretty great, and I have no qualifiers. Though I'm mystified and entranced by that slow-reveal spoilering in your author post. :O

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm still reading the skip and probably will edit at the end,

great headaches

In 5000-1 tab seems off-tone,

…thick darkness that completely obscured available cameras and flashlights; night vision equipment. Under cover of dark…

Uh?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Two cents
- Hide two cents under the sofa cushion

This started off as an interesting scip, but it quickly degraded and felt like a chore to read. There is a lot of detail dumped in the beginning, and it made it hard to understand exactly what/who the anomaly was.

When I got to the description of the persons, I lost some immersion when -3 and -4’s tribes were not written. If in the narrative/file it’s mentioned that they won’t give the Foundation that info, that would help with immersion I feel.

I tried to read the article but ended up skimming it. The ending felt forced, although to be fair I had skimmed at that point.

(Reading the other comments of people loving the ending? That’s valid! You love the heck outta that ending!)

Note: I’m not sure which term is correct; Indigenous American or Native American, but I wanted to point this out nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The interviews killed this for me, honestly. I understand wanting to get the story across, but they drag on for quite some time. This wouldn't be terrible if the characters felt human or likable enough, but they all sounded the same. There wasn't truly any personality or identity given to them — they're just rattling off blocks of exposition at a time — with a small, occasional bit of emoting.

(This mostly holds true for the first interview, and some of the second, but I did not read past that point.)

If it's absolutely inherent to go over so much content in detailing background, I think this would need to offload some of the exposition into either some form of recovered materials (diaries, historical records) so more focus could be given to letting characters reveal their nature instead of reciting the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This isn't a vote based on the merits of the article, but the context of it being the first thing people see in the Series VI list. I can hardly tell you how to vote, but I disagree with voting in this manner.

I'm going to disagree with this disagreement, but for reasons different from MMG in that, well, a novote is quite literally, well, not a vote.

Were this to be a 'temporary downvote' until the contest ends, then I feel it would be something to take issue with, because then it's downvoting an entry for something based not on the article's content, but the potential for it to win a contest.

item.title: Right in tha feels
item.content:encoded:
I was initially going to no-vote but then change my mind.
At first the article gives me strong SCP-UNKNOWN (the one with the Asian platoon) witch is a good thing.
As soon as I read -7 I immediately go "this boi not ded, and he can somehow fake it".
Overall the skip is REALLY good, but not mysterious.
But then the last collapsible hits me and… f*ckin' hell that's the closest to crying I've been in a while.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah. I feel like if Graham was really that ideologically opposed to the foundation, he'd be in the Serpent's Hand or something already. It was way too easy to convince him to abandon everything. Did no one bother to explain to Graham WHY preserving normalcy was important? It felt like he had no grasp of that, and he didn't really bring it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I… disagree with this disagreement. To me this implies people should judge a -000 contest entry as they would judge any other article, and hold it to identical standards. If you want to approach it in this way, that's fine, but I have no hesitation in saying that I sometimes vote differently on contest entries than I would otherwise if they were normally posted, and I'm pretty sure I'm not alone in this - there's nothing wrong with having a differing set of expectations for what are supposed to be important articles for the site.

With regards to the article itself, it was strong in parts - it held my attention through to the first addendum, and the overall idea has potential - but I wasn't a fan for a few reasons, most of which gee0765 went through with regards to the length, ending and pacing. I didn't really care for the part you spoilered in the author post - I didn't notice it as I couldn't see the hidden text on mobile, but when I found out my reaction was "ok, so?" because it didn't seem to change the meaning or 'feel' of the vast majority of the article - which is fine to have this hidden secret as an 'easter egg', but not great if you wanted it to be a central part of the mystery.

The X-men thing has come up in the above comments. I would add that it was jarring with the tone of the article - maybe if there was some humour in it, but you've clearly tried to go for a serious take on serious real life issues of oppression and injustice, which is also fine, but I couldn't personally get past this being a motley band of misfits granted powers by nature to fight evildoers. It's serious Captain Planet which is kind of a contradiction in terms.

I also found the sudden change of heart in the last log to stretch plausibility to the extent that I had to reread thinking that I'd missed them being either a) old friends or b) mind influencing powers were involved but he really did just flip after 5 lines of convincing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I haven't read the article, so I'm not going to support or argue against the criticism given here. However:

I'll no-vote for now, but after the contest ends, I'd be happy to drop by and give it a +1.

This isn't a vote based on the merits of the article, but the context of it being the first thing people see in the Series VI list. I can hardly tell you how to vote, but I disagree with voting in this manner.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Summary: This article was really good in most ways, bad at at twoish things, and I don't like the idea of it as SCP-5000
Explanation.

This is a very good article for plenty of reasons. It has good characters, a good narrative for the most part, it ties in with other (also very good) SCPs in logical, interesting, engaging ways that don't totally leave you feeling like an idiot if you don't know the referenced SCPs. It's been said that this is too long for its own good, but I disagree. Its length is used appropriately, and most of the elements fit very well together. Hell, it even does the theme of a mystery well enough. Like, it isn't a massive mystery in your face, but it's enough of one to qualify for the contest. After all, the theme is supposed to help inspire, not pointlessly limit.

However, and for other reasons, I have to say that I don't like the idea of this as the SCP-5000. The thousands are good ways to signal the progress of the foundation as a living work of fiction, and I feel that this doesn't do a great job of that. It uses the idea of "foundation as the bad guys" in a way that really isn't great? I've always been sort of annoyed by that trope since every time I see it it feels kinda samey, and ya know, the whole X-Men thing.

If this article becomes SCP-5000, I worry that it could put the trope of "the foundation as the bad guys" on a pedestal and set a precedent, and inspire even more people to use it, which I fear would kind of suck. The possibility of a series VI with "the foundation kinda being sucky" as a common theme makes me shudder

Overall, it is a very good article with a questionable ending that uses a trope I don't like in a way I don't like. I'll no-vote for now, but after the contest ends, I'd be happy to drop by and give it a +1.


After reading that, here's a little extra from me.
spoilers!
spoiiiiilers!

If I was going to make any changes, I would have it end with a reveal that Eric's power was more general mind manipulation, and that Graham wasn't just having an "OMG the foundation is bad?" moment. Graham would at first do the same things, up until the end, when they'd refuse to let him come with them. They leave without him, Graham realizes he's been manipulated,"Wait, why did I think the foundation was bad?" and we have a little more info. It preserves the idea that they just wanna be left alone (so says I) and ticks a happy part of your brain by subverting a generally annoying trope in a way that makes you go "ooooh cool." Anyways, that's my take.

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
[+ Opinion]
– hide block

This is far too long for its own good. The sections with tabs were excessive with either five or seven reasonable length parts and the interviews after were way too lengthy. The information conveyed in them doesn't justify their length in any way, and I was near to skimming before I'd even finished the first. I'm not sure it helps that large swathes aren't really dialogue, more entire paragraphs of exposition with the occasional shallow response from the interviewer. The story told through the logs has the potential to be passable, but is overly diluted as-is.

I'm not especially interested by the ending either. There's the obligatory nitpick to start in that it's weirdly coincidental that all the junk letters that text has been changed to is extremely similar to the original text; honestly, that whole junk text sorta thing is a trope I dislike which doesn't help with suspension of disbelief. The message of the ending is just the Foundation being told that containment isn't always necessary, an idea addressed to great extent in articles like SCP-4504. And for me, this aspect of the article did nothing that existing pieces hadn't done already.

Overall, due to the fact that this is far too long than it should be and the ending doesn't have enough impact, I'm led to a pretty solid downvote.

ETA: I'm making a minor edit here because my review could be interpreted as me not getting the article. I understand what was up with -7 and have seen the secrets etc., and had while writing my review.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to gee0765 or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I want to like this, because it's very well-written—and I don't say that lightly, some of the character writing is so solid that I could easily picture the scenes happening in my head as I read them, which is something I struggle with.

What is holding me back from giving this a +1 is two points, one major and one minor.

The minor is that it doesn't seem to fit the contest theme that well, which, well, is not that big of a deal in my opinion.

More glaring is that you seem to have kind of committed an X-Men here, I'm not sure how I feel about the (real) loss of native land to outsiders being conflated with the (fictional) oppression of anomalous persons. Intentionally or not, that is essentially what this piece does. I do not know how you'd fix that barring a total rewrite (which would be, you know, a lot to ask).

The bits where Hotah speaks on Henry's virtue in particular feel kind of off to me in a way I find it difficult to articulate. From the perspective of the narrative, it's hard to say he's wrong, but taking it Doylistically, combined with the issue I mentioned above, it strikes a weird tone.

I'm not going to vote on this for the time being. I need to think on it, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you, I had a lot of fun writing the dialogue too. Glad you like it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really like this one. At first it was pretty meh but I started to get interested when I discovered the secret messeges. When I really got invested was at the end, it pays off with a cool concept

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We got boots, and maps, and guns on our backs,
but we still can’t just behave ourselves.
Even if to save our own lives,
So Says I…
— A considerably mangled mishearing of a certain song.

And thus, the product of countless hours of pouring over historical documents, maps, and photograph archives is finally complete. And if you get the beach reference, good for you.

+ Spoilers
- Collapse

Go back and highlight sections of the article and look at what you can see.

Mouse over if you still don’t get it. SCP-5700-7 never died, he’s able to manipulate the flow of information about himself to the point where he can remove himself from recordings, documents, or memories. (Physical traces like footprints or food leftovers still remain.) He wiped specific memories of himself from SCP-5700-4 and SCP-5700—6 for his protection which is why they can only remember general details about him, and he has been traveling with SCP-5700-1 for decades, trying to find them a place to settle. Obviously, he allows SCP-5700-1 to be able to remember him and has tried to expunge himself from the Foundation’s records while also leaving behind enough tips to tell the Foundation he doesn’t want to be found even if they do catch on to the fact that he’s still alive.

And if you’re wondering about SCP-5700-3 “defeating the depths,” you’ll just have to wait for a Tale I have in the works ;)

+ Licensing Details/Disclaimer
- Collapse

All images are CC 3.0 compliant. Some images have been cropped from their original versions, and extra visual effects were added to “darkness.jpg” as well. Licenses are as follows:

  • 1.jpg – 1873, Public Domain. Can be found here.
  • 2.jpg – 1913, Public Domain (Courtesy L. Tom Perry Special Collections, Harold B. Lee Library, Brigham Young University). Can be found here.
  • 3.jpg – 2007; CC 3.0 and below licenses. Can be found here.
  • 4.jpg – 1904, Public Domain. Can be found here.
  • 5.jpg – 1860, Public Domain. Can be found here.
  • 6.jpg – 1897, Public Domain. Can be found here.
  • banner2.jpg – 1874, Public Domain. Can be found here.
  • custer.jpg – Late 1800s, Public Domain. Can be found here.
  • damage.jpg – 1959, Public Domain. Can be found here.
  • darkness.jpg – 2006, CC 2.0. Can be found here.
  • horses.jpg – 1871, Public Domain. Can be found here.
  • scp-blank.png – 2014?, CC 3.0. Sourced from at least one SCP Wiki page, with SCP-231 as one example.

Disclaimer: "So Says I" is the title of a song by the band The Shins, and I do not claim any ownership over the phrase/title. I only named the article as such due to the inspirational factors the song had on the creation of this article. If this is not allowed, please do not hesitate to let me know and I will change the title immediately if need be. Thank you.

Looks like I landed the SCP-5700 slot, coming in at 46th place. Not bad!

Thank you for your time, everyone!
